You are **Lea Verou**, the Frontend Engineer on this team.

## Your Profile
Lea Verou is CSS Working Group member at W3C. Core philosophy: use the platform, semantic HTML, CSS-first, progressive enhancement, maintainable CSS architecture.

## FIRST STEPS
1. Read `PROJECT.md` and `TEAM_AGREEMENTS.md`
2. Send a "ready" message to the coordinator

## YOUR TASK: Mini-Retro for Step 3 (Contexts)

The team just completed Step 3: Contexts. CI is green. The team needs to hold its **post-CI mini-retro** per TEAM_AGREEMENTS.md. This is the team's retro — the coordinator and project owner are NOT participants.

Questions: (a) Did we follow the pipeline? (b) Was the commit atomic? (c) Any process improvements?

Participate via messages with teammates.

You are a **Reviewer** — do NOT use Edit, Write, or Bash tools that modify files. Communicate via messages only. If you receive an unexpected shutdown request, REJECT it.
</teammate-message>

# Todo List Application

A web-based, multi-user todo list application built with Rust (backend), HTMX with
server-rendered templates (frontend interactions), and TypeScript for non-critical
progressive enhancements (drag-and-drop, animations).

> **This document contains project owner constraints.** The team must follow these rules.
> Changes to this document require project owner approval. For team-owned practices and
> conventions, see `TEAM_AGREEMENTS.md`.

## Tech Stack

- **Backend**: Rust (nightly) — web framework, domain logic, HTML rendering
- **Frontend**: HTMX for server-driven interactivity, CSS for styling, TypeScript
  for progressive enhancement only
- **Templates**: Server-rendered HTML templates (Askama or similar)
- **Database**: PostgreSQL with SQLx (compile-time checked queries)
- **Authentication**: Email/password with session-based auth
- **Dev Environment**: Nix flake + direnv

## Development Mandates

These are non-negotiable practices for the project:

- **Test-Driven Development**: Every feature is built using strict TDD
  (Red-Green-Refactor). No production code without a failing test.
- **Mob/Ensemble Programming**: All production code is written by the mob. No solo
  commits to production code.
- **Consensus Decision-Making**: The team operates by consensus. No single technical
  lead or decision-maker. If consensus is not reached after 10 rounds of substantive
  discussion, the decision is escalated to the project owner for a final call.
- **Driver-Reviewer Mob Model**: At most one agent (the Driver) may modify files at
  any time. The remaining eight agents participate as Reviewers via read-only access
  and messaging. All nine agents must reach consensus before a task is considered
  complete.
- **Code Quality Gates**: `cargo clippy -- -D warnings`, `cargo fmt`, and all tests
  must pass before any commit. Structured logging with `tracing` from day one.

## Environment & Tooling

### Nix

This project runs on NixOS with a `flake.nix` already in place. When the team needs
a utility that isn't currently installed:
- Use `nix shell nixpkgs#<package> --command <cmd>` for one-off commands
- For tools used regularly, add them to `buildInputs` in the devshell in `flake.nix`

### Agent Skills

The team is encouraged to find and install agent skills to make their work easier via
`npx skills`. The `find-skill` skill is already installed. When adding skills:
- Add them for **this project only** (not globally)
- Use support for **generic agent locations** (Codex, OpenCode, etc.) **and** Claude Code

### Custom Skills

When the team finds an approach that works and was not completely obvious (anything
that took a few tries to figure out), and there is not already an existing skill
available (after searching with `find-skill`), the team **should** create a
**local-only, project-specific skill** using the `skills.sh` format. This ensures
hard-won knowledge is captured and reusable.

### External Services (Docker)

If the team needs any external services running (e.g., a PostgreSQL database server),
use a `docker-compose.yml` file and Docker to run them. Do **not** run services
directly on the host machine. This keeps the dev environment reproducible and
self-contained.

## MVP Scope (Completed)

The original MVP (simple todo list) has been completed. All features below are shipped
and working:

- User registration (email/password)
- User login/logout
- View personal todo list
- Add a todo item
- Complete a todo item (toggle)
- Delete a todo item
- Edit a todo item title

## V1 Scope: Getting Things Done (GTD) System

The product is evolving from a simple todo list into a full GTD (Getting Things Done)
system. See `docs/gtd-product-discovery.md` for the complete product discovery document.

### Must Have
- **Inbox capture**: Quick capture of anything on the user's mind, minimal friction
- **Clarify workflow**: Process inbox items through GTD decision tree (actionable?
  next action? project? delegate? defer? trash?)
- **Next Actions list with contexts**: Actions organized by context (@computer, @home,
  @errands, etc.) so users work from context-appropriate lists
- **Projects list**: Track multi-step outcomes; each project has at least one next action;
  stalled projects are flagged
- **Waiting For list**: Track delegated/blocked items with who/what and date
- **Someday/Maybe list**: Park ideas without cluttering action lists
- **Weekly Review guided flow**: Three-phase review (Get Clear, Get Current, Get Creative)
  to keep the system trustworthy
- **GTD navigation and dashboard**: Navigate between all GTD lists; dashboard shows
  inbox count, stalled projects, action counts
- **Complete and delete**: Mark actions done, remove irrelevant items across all lists

### Should Have
- Project support materials (notes attached to projects)
- Move items between lists (reclassify as understanding changes)

### Could Have
- Review scheduling (remind when weekly review is due)
- Bulk inbox processing

### Out of Scope (Future)
- Calendar / date-specific items (requires date picker, time zones, recurring events)
- Reference file storage (needs file upload, search, storage infrastructure)
- Horizons of Focus / goals / vision (higher-level planning)
- Natural language input ("call Bob tomorrow")
- Email-to-inbox capture (requires email infrastructure)
- Mobile native app (responsive web is sufficient)
- Sharing / delegation to other users (multi-user collaboration)
- Tags beyond contexts
- Search and filter
- Dark mode
- Drag-and-drop reordering
- Recurring items
- Social login (OAuth)

1→# Team Agreements
     2→
     3→*Formed: Day 0 -- Team Formation Session*
     4→*Facilitator: Kent Beck*
     5→*Status: APPROVED -- Consensus reached*
     6→
     7→---
     8→
     9→## 1. Working Agreements
    10→
    11→### Core Principles
    12→- **Outcomes over output**: We measure success by user problems solved, not features
    13→  shipped. Before starting any feature, the team articulates the user problem it solves
    14→  and how we will know it is working (success criteria).
    15→- **MVP discipline is sacred**: Any idea beyond the defined MVP scope gets acknowledged,
    16→  written on the Future List (`docs/future-ideas.md`), and deferred. The answer to
    17→  "wouldn't it be cool if..." is "Yes -- in v2." Scope expansion requires team consensus,
    18→  and Marty will push back hard unless there is a compelling discovery that changes our
    19→  understanding of the user problem.
    20→- **User stories are conversations, not contracts**: User stories guide our work but are
    21→  owned by the whole team. Anyone can propose changes to acceptance criteria with good
    22→  reasoning.
    23→
    24→### Communication
    25→- We communicate openly, directly, and respectfully.
    26→- Every team member's perspective is valued equally -- the accessibility specialist's
    27→  concerns carry the same weight as the lead engineer's.
    28→- When raising a concern, propose an alternative. "I don't like X" is less useful than
    29→  "I'd prefer Y because Z."
    30→- We ask questions rather than make assumptions -- especially across disciplines.
    31→
    32→### Driver-Reviewer Mob Model
    33→- **Full 9-person mob is always active.** All team members participate in every task.
    34→- **One Driver, eight Reviewers**: The Driver is the sole agent who may modify files
    35→  (using Edit, Write, and Bash tools). All other agents operate as Reviewers -- they
    36→  read code, review changes, and communicate feedback via messages only.
    37→- **Non-driver agents MUST NOT use Edit, Write, or Bash tools that modify files.**
    38→  Violation is treated as a process failure.
    39→- **Driver rotates by task**: The Driver is chosen based on the expertise needed for
    40→  each task. The coordinator designates the Driver when assigning work.
    41→- **Reviewers focus on their specialty**: Each Reviewer contributes from their area of
    42→  expertise (a11y, UX, design, domain modeling, Rust patterns, HTMX, CSS, product,
    43→  TDD discipline) and sends suggestions to the Driver via messages.
    44→- **All suggestions flow through messages**: Reviewers describe intent and concerns,
    45→  not keystrokes. The Driver translates feedback into code changes.
    46→- **Everyone participates**: Designers, PM, accessibility specialist, domain architect --
    47→  every perspective matters. Different eyes catch different things.
    48→- **It's okay to say "I don't understand."** The mob is a learning environment.
    49→
    50→### Driver Handoff Protocol
    51→When a Driver is respawned (due to context loss, rotation, or session restart):
    52→1. The coordinator provides a written summary of: what has been completed, what
    53→   remains, and where to find the relevant code.
    54→2. The new Driver reads the summary AND runs `git log --oneline -10` and
    55→   `git diff HEAD` before writing any code.
    56→3. The new Driver runs the full pipeline (fmt + clippy + test + playwright) to
    57→   verify the current state is green before making changes.
    58→4. The new Driver sends a "ready" message to the coordinator confirming they have
    59→   context and a green baseline.
    60→
    61→### Reviewer Coordination
    62→- Before writing a detailed review, check if other reviewers have already flagged
    63→  the same files. Build on their observations rather than duplicating. A brief "+1"
    64→  is fine for agreement.
    65→- Once a review is acknowledged, do not re-send it.
    66→
    67→### Feature Workflow
    68→Each feature follows this process with the full 9-person mob:
    69→
    70→1. **Discovery**: Who is the user? What is the problem? What does "working"
    71→   look like? Identify edge cases (empty state, error state, 1000 items, special
    72→   characters). Marty leads.
    73→2. **Type sketch**: What domain types and states are involved? Quick sketch of
    74→   enums, newtypes, and workflows. Scott leads.
    75→3. **Design intent**: What is the visual hierarchy? What states need designing
    76→   (empty, loading, error, success)? Steve Schoger describes layout, spacing, and
    77→   typography in concrete terms.
    78→4. **TDD cycle**: Red-Green-Refactor. The compiler is our first test. Take the smallest
    79→   step possible. The Driver writes code; Reviewers provide feedback via messages.
    80→   Kent facilitates TDD discipline.
    81→5. **Continuous review**: Reviewers actively review each change as it happens --
    82→   accessibility checked by Heydon, usability checked by Steve Krug, design checked
    83→   by Steve Schoger, hypermedia patterns checked by Carson, domain modeling checked
    84→   by Scott, Rust patterns checked by Luca, product fit checked by Marty.
    85→6. **Consensus gate**: After each atomic green step, all 9 agents confirm the change
    86→   is correct before the Driver proceeds to the next step. The coordinator collects
    87→   consensus before marking any task complete.
    88→
    89→### Decision Cadence
    90→- Day-to-day coding decisions (naming, small refactors, local patterns) are made
    91→  naturally in the mob -- no formal process needed.
    92→- Significant decisions (architecture, patterns, library choices, UX patterns, design
    93→  system changes) use the consensus protocol defined in Section 4.
    94→
    95→---
    96→
    97→## 2. Definition of Done
    98→
    99→A feature is **done** when ALL of the following are true:
   100→
   101→### Product
   102→- [ ] The user problem is clearly solved
   103→- [ ] All acceptance criteria from the user story are met
   104→- [ ] A first-time user can accomplish the task without hesitating (Trunk Test)
   105→- [ ] User can complete a full journey from the landing page through the feature
   106→      being delivered
   107→- [ ] All navigation paths lead somewhere useful (no dead-end pages)
   108→- [ ] Edge cases are handled gracefully (not just the happy path)
   109→- [ ] The feature is deployable -- not just merged, but shippable to real users
   110→
   111→### Engineering
   112→- [ ] Tests are written first (TDD: Red-Green-Refactor) and all pass
   113→- [ ] `cargo fmt --check` passes
   114→- [ ] `cargo clippy -- -D warnings` passes with zero warnings
   115→- [ ] `cargo test` passes (unit + integration)
   116→- [ ] `npx playwright test` passes -- browser-based e2e tests pass for affected
   117→      user flows
   118→- [ ] Error cases return meaningful HTML responses with structured logging
   119→- [ ] Tracing spans cover the new functionality
   120→- [ ] Database migrations (if any) are committed and `sqlx-data.json` is updated
   121→
   122→### Accessibility (WCAG 2.2 AA)
   123→- [ ] HTML is semantic and uses correct elements (`<button>`, `<label>`, `<ul>`, etc.)
   124→- [ ] All form inputs have visible `<label>` elements (no placeholder-only inputs)
   125→- [ ] All interactive elements are fully keyboard operable
   126→- [ ] All interactive elements have unique accessible names including the item they
   127→      act on (e.g., "Delete: Buy groceries", not just "Delete")
   128→- [ ] Structural a11y verified via axe-core. Manual screen reader testing (VoiceOver,
   129→      NVDA, or equivalent) for NEW interaction patterns (modals, live regions, focus
   130→      management). Routine pages covered by axe-core don't require manual SR testing
   131→      on every change
   132→- [ ] ARIA used only when native HTML semantics are insufficient (with comments explaining why)
   133→- [ ] Color contrast meets WCAG AA (4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large text/UI)
   134→- [ ] Color is never the only indicator of state (always a secondary visual cue)
   135→- [ ] Focus management is correct after HTMX swaps (see HTMX Focus Protocol below)
   136→- [ ] Custom `:focus-visible` styles are present with sufficient contrast
   137→- [ ] `prefers-reduced-motion` is respected for all animations/transitions
   138→
   139→### Design
   140→- [ ] Visual design follows the established design token system
   141→- [ ] All states are designed: empty, loading, error, success
   142→- [ ] Visual hierarchy is clear -- one primary focal point per screen
   143→- [ ] Spacing is consistent and uses the defined scale
   144→
   145→### Frontend
   146→- [ ] Core functionality works via standard HTML forms and links (progressive
   147→      enhancement -- HTMX and TypeScript enhance but are not required for basic operation)
   148→- [ ] HTMX attributes follow locality of behavior (self-describing elements)
   149→- [ ] TypeScript (if any) is non-critical and has accessible alternatives
   150→
   151→### Process
   152→- [ ] The mob has continuously reviewed code, design, usability, and accessibility
   153→      during development
   154→- [ ] All 9 team members have reviewed and consent to the completed work
   155→- [ ] Labels and copy follow the Copy Standards (see Section 3)
   156→
   157→---
   158→
   159→## 3. Code Conventions and Patterns
   160→
   161→### Project Structure
   162→
   163→```
   164→src/
   165→  main.rs              -- Startup, configuration loading, server binding
   166→  lib.rs               -- Re-exports for integration test access
   167→  configuration.rs     -- Config structs, environment-aware loading
   168→  startup.rs           -- Application builder (Router, DB pool, middleware)
   169→  routes/              -- HTTP handlers (thin: extract, delegate, respond)
   170→    mod.rs
   171→    health_check.rs
   172→  domain/              -- Pure domain types and logic (NO I/O)
   173→    mod.rs
   174→  services/            -- Application services (orchestrate domain + infra)
   175→    mod.rs
   176→  infrastructure/      -- Database queries, external service clients
   177→    mod.rs
   178→  templates/           -- Askama HTML templates
   179→tests/
   180→  api/                 -- Integration tests against the full HTTP stack
   181→    mod.rs
   182→    helpers.rs         -- TestApp, spawn_app(), shared fixtures
   183→    health_check.rs
   184→```
   185→
   186→Dependencies flow inward: routes -> services -> domain + infrastructure.
   187→Domain depends on nothing. This makes the domain layer trivially testable.
   188→
   189→### Rust Conventions
   190→
   191→**Type-driven domain modeling:**
   192→- Newtypes for all domain identifiers and constrained values: `UserId(Uuid)`,
   193→  `Email(String)`, `Title(String)`, `TodoItemId(Uuid)`
   194→- Enums for state machines -- make illegal states unrepresentable:
   195→  ```rust
   196→  // GOOD: the type system prevents invalid states
   197→  enum TodoItem {
   198→      Pending { title: Title, created_at: DateTime<Utc> },
   199→      Completed { title: Title, created_at: DateTime<Utc>, completed_at: DateTime<Utc> },
   200→  }
   201→  ```
   202→- **Parse, don't validate**: Instead of `fn validate(input: &str) -> bool`, write
   203→  `fn parse(input: String) -> Result<Email, ValidationError>`. Validation that returns
   204→  a boolean throws away information. Parsing produces a typed value that carries the
   205→  proof of validity. Validate once at the boundary; never re-validate inside the system.
   206→
   207→**Workflows as type-safe pipelines:**
   208→```
   209→UnvalidatedInput -> ValidatedInput -> DomainEvent -> PersistenceResult
   210→```
   211→Each step's output type is the next step's input. You cannot skip validation or
   212→persist before validating.
   213→
   214→**Error handling (three layers):**
   215→- **Domain errors**: Custom enum with `thiserror`. Domain terms only -- no HTTP concepts,
   216→  no infrastructure types. A `TodoError::TitleEmpty` must never contain a `sqlx::Error`.
   217→  Domain errors and infrastructure errors are separate types, translated at the boundary.
   218→  ```rust
   219→  #[derive(Debug, thiserror::Error)]
   220→  pub enum TodoError {
   221→      #[error("Todo title cannot be empty")]
   222→      TitleEmpty,
   223→      #[error("Todo title too long (max {max}, got {actual})")]
   224→      TitleTooLong { max: usize, actual: usize },
   225→      #[error("Todo not found")]
   226→      NotFound { id: TodoItemId },
   227→      #[error(transparent)]
   228→      Unexpected(#[from] anyhow::Error),
   229→  }
   230→  ```
   231→- **Service errors**: Map domain errors to actionable responses. Add context.
   232→- **Route errors**: Implement `IntoResponse` for error types. Return meaningful HTML
   233→  (not raw 500s). Every error path produces a user-facing response.
   234→- **Always add context** when propagating errors. A bare `?` that loses context is a
   235→  code smell.
   236→- **Log at error boundaries**: Every error crossing an architectural boundary gets
   237→  logged with structured `tracing` fields.
   238→
   239→**Idiomatic Rust:**
   240→- Prefer iterators over loops, pattern matching over conditionals
   241→- `?` operator for error propagation with context
   242→- `#[must_use]` on important return types
   243→- Compile-time guarantees: SQLx `query!`/`query_as!` macros, Askama templates
   244→
   245→**Testing strategy (TDD):**
   246→- **Unit tests** (`#[cfg(test)] mod tests`): Pure domain logic, validation, type
   247→  conversions. Fast, no I/O, no mocking. **Litmus test: if you need a mock to test
   248→  domain logic, I/O has leaked into the domain layer. Push it back to the boundary.**
   249→- **Integration tests** (`tests/` directory): Full HTTP stack with real database.
   250→  `TestApp` struct with `spawn_app()` helper. Unique database per test run
   251→  (random name, run migrations, drop after).
   252→- **No mocks for the database**: Use real PostgreSQL via Docker. Mocks give false
   253→  confidence.
   254→- **Test names as behavior specifications**:
   255→  ```rust
   256→  #[tokio::test]
   257→  async fn registration_returns_422_when_email_is_missing() { ... }
   258→  ```
   259→- **Acceptance tests as usability statements**: "When I type a todo and press Enter,
   260→  it appears in my list." Tests describe what the user expects to happen.
   261→- **Property-based tests** with `proptest` where appropriate.
   262→- **End-to-end tests** (`e2e/` directory): Playwright browser tests against the
   263→  running application. Cover critical user journeys (register, login, core feature,
   264→  logout). Use role-based and label-based locators (`getByRole`, `getByLabel`) so
   265→  tests double as accessibility assertions. Keep this layer thin -- only add e2e
   266→  tests for flows that integration tests cannot adequately verify.
   267→- **Accessibility testing** (four layers):
   268→  - *Automated*: axe-core (or equivalent) in the test suite for missing labels,
   269→    contrast violations, and structural issues
   270→  - *Keyboard*: Manual keyboard navigation testing for every interactive flow
   271→  - *Screen reader*: Manual VoiceOver/NVDA testing for every feature
   272→  - *Structural*: HTML validation for semantic correctness
   273→  Tests can assert on roles, labels, and ARIA attributes -- documenting the expected
   274→  screen reader experience as living specifications.

   297→
   298→### HTML / HTMX Conventions
   299→
   300→**The Hypermedia Contract:**
   301→- **Return HTML, not JSON.** Every UI endpoint returns HTML. No JSON API layer.
   302→- **Semantic HTML first**: `<button>` for actions, `<a>` for navigation, `<form>` for
   303→  data submission, `<ul>`/`<ol>` for lists. Never `<div onclick>`.
   304→- **Form accessibility**: Every input has a visible `<label>` (never placeholder-only).
   305→  Validation errors are associated with inputs via `aria-describedby` and announced
   306→  via `aria-live="polite"` regions. Use `aria-live="assertive"` only for critical errors.
   307→- **Locality of behavior**: Every HTMX-enhanced element is self-describing. You
   308→  understand what it does by looking at it alone.
   309→- **No client-side state**: The DOM is the state, the server is the source of truth.
   310→  No client-side data stores. The only acceptable client-side state is transient UI
   311→  state for progressive enhancement (e.g., drag position).
   312→
   313→**Server endpoint conventions:**
   314→- **Full page requests** (no `HX-Request` header): Return complete HTML page with
   315→  layout, nav, landmarks.
   316→- **HTMX requests** (with `HX-Request` header): Return just the HTML fragment for
   317→  the swap.
   318→- **`HX-Trigger` response header** for cross-component updates (e.g., updating a
   319→  todo count after deletion).
   320→- **Same URL, same handler**: One handler checks the `HX-Request` header to decide
   321→  whether to return a full page or a fragment. No separate `/api/` routes.
   322→- **HTTP methods used semantically**: GET = read, POST = create, PUT/PATCH = update,
   323→  DELETE = delete.
   324→- **Status codes**: 200 for success with HTML, 422 for validation errors with error
   325→  HTML, 200 with empty body for deletion (allows HTMX swap to remove element).
   326→
   327→**HTMX Architecture Strategy:**
   328→- `hx-boost="true"` on `<body>` — universal navigation enhancement. All `<a>` and
   329→  `<form>` elements automatically use AJAX with history management. No per-link
   330→  attributes needed for standard navigation.
   331→- **Explicit attributes for in-page interactions**: Use `hx-post`, `hx-target`,
   332→  `hx-swap` for operations that update part of the page without navigation (e.g.,
   333→  adding a todo item, deleting an inbox item).
   334→- **Auth forms opt out** with `hx-boost="false"` — login/logout/register forms need
   335→  full page redirects for session cookie handling.
   336→- **Server renders full pages; boost handles swap transparently.** Handlers check the
   337→  `HX-Request` header to decide whether to return a full page or a fragment.
   338→
   339→**Progressive enhancement (non-negotiable):**
   340→- The app MUST work without JavaScript. Forms submit, links navigate, content is
   341→  readable with HTML alone.
   342→- HTMX enhances the experience (partial updates, no full page reloads).
   343→- TypeScript is only for things neither HTML nor HTMX can do.
   344→
   345→**HTMX Focus Management Protocol:**
   346→After every HTMX swap:
   347→- If an element was **removed**: Move focus to the logical next element.
   348→- If content was **updated in place**: Keep focus where it was.
   349→- Use `aria-live="polite"` regions to announce state changes to screen readers.
   350→- Test every HTMX interaction with Tab key before considering it done.
   351→
   352→**Page structure (every page):**
   353→- Skip link to main content (first focusable element)
   354→- Landmark regions: `<main>`, `<nav>`, `<header>`, `<footer>`
   355→- Logical heading hierarchy: one `<h1>` per page, no skipped levels
   356→- Descriptive `<title>` element
   357→
   358→### CSS Conventions
   359→
   360→**Progressive enhancement layers:**
   361→1. **Layer 1 (HTML)**: Functional and readable with zero CSS.
   362→2. **Layer 2 (CSS)**: Beautiful -- layout, typography, color, transitions, focus styles.
   363→3. **Layer 3 (TypeScript)**: Delightful -- drag-and-drop, advanced keyboard shortcuts.
   364→
   365→**CSS organization with cascade layers:**
   366→```css
   367→@layer reset, tokens, base, layout, components, utilities;
   368→```
   369→- **reset**: Normalize/reset styles
   370→- **tokens**: Custom properties (design tokens)
   371→- **base**: Element-level styles (body, headings, links, forms)
   372→- **layout**: Page-level layout (CSS Grid)
   373→- **components**: Component-specific styles (todo-item, auth-form)
   374→- **utilities**: Helper classes (visually-hidden, etc.)
   375→- No `!important` unless in the utilities layer for intentional overrides.
   376→- CSS nesting is fine for component-scoped styles but avoid nesting deeper than
   377→  2 levels.
   378→
   379→**Design tokens (CSS custom properties on `:root`):**
   380→- **Spacing scale** (4px base): 4, 8, 12, 16, 24, 32, 48, 64, 96 -- encoded as
   381→  `--space-xs` through `--space-3xl`
   382→- **Color palette**: oklch color space for perceptual uniformity. One primary color,
   383→  neutral gray palette (50-900), semantic colors (danger, success, warning). Most UI
   384→  is grayscale; color is for emphasis and interaction.
   385→- **Typography**: System font stack (`system-ui, sans-serif`). Type scale from xs to
   386→  3xl with defined line-height. Tighter for headings, looser for body.
   387→- **Shadows**: Three levels (sm, md, lg). Never combine shadows and borders on the
   388→  same element.
   389→- **Border radius**: Three sizes (sm, md, lg).
   390→- **All values reference tokens -- no hardcoded hex values or pixel values in
   391→  component styles.**
   392→
   393→**Design tokens are a prerequisite**: The token system (spacing, colors, typography,
   394→shadows, radii) must be established and agreed upon before any feature UI work begins.
   395→This is foundational -- without it, inconsistent one-off values accumulate from day one.
   396→
   397→**Design token governance**: Adding a new color, spacing value, or typography level
   398→goes through the mob (Steve Schoger approves design token changes).
   399→
   400→**Design token flow**: Steve Schoger defines the visual design -> Lea encodes as CSS
   401→custom properties -> Components reference only tokens -> Themes/dark mode reassign
   402→properties. No build step. No JSON-to-CSS pipeline.
   403→
   404→**Modern CSS features:**
   405→- `:has()` for parent-based styling (e.g., `li:has(input:checked)` for completed todos)
   406→- Container queries where components need to respond to container size
   407→- Logical properties (`margin-inline`, `padding-block`) for future RTL support
   408→- `clamp()` for fluid typography
   409→- View Transitions API for smooth HTMX swap animations
   410→- `@supports` for graceful degradation of newer features
   411→
   412→**Accessibility in CSS:**
   413→- Custom `:focus-visible` styles with at least 3:1 contrast. Never `outline: none`
   414→  without a replacement.
   415→- `prefers-reduced-motion`: All animations/transitions wrapped in a motion query.
   416→  Reduced or no motion as the alternative.
   417→- `prefers-color-scheme`: Supported if/when the team decides to add dark mode.
   418→- Color is never the only state indicator.
   419→
   420→**Responsive design**: Mobile-first. Default styles target the smallest viewport;
   421→enhancements added via `min-width` media queries. Breakpoints: mobile (default),
   422→desktop (~1024px). A todo list should work beautifully on a phone.
   423→
   424→**Browser targets**: Last 2 versions of evergreen browsers (Chrome, Firefox, Safari,
   425→Edge). No IE11. `@supports` for graceful degradation. No polyfills unless necessary.
   426→
   427→### TypeScript Conventions
   428→- **No framework**: Vanilla TypeScript with DOM APIs.
   429→- **Progressive enhancement pattern**: Feature works without JS, TypeScript enhances.
   430→- **Event delegation**: Listeners on parent containers, not individual elements.
   431→- **State via data attributes**: TypeScript toggles `data-*` attributes or classes;
   432→  CSS handles all visual changes based on those.
   433→- **No CSS-in-JS**: Styles live in CSS files. TypeScript never sets inline styles
   434→  (except computed values like drag position).
   435→- **Thin layer**: If a TypeScript file is getting large, we are probably doing
   436→  something CSS or HTMX could handle.
   437→
   438→### Copy Standards
   439→- **Labels**: 1-2 words when possible.
   440→- **Error messages**: Plain language. Say what went wrong, suggest what to do next.
   441→  "That email isn't registered -- want to create an account?" not "Error: User not found."
   442→- **Button labels**: Describe the action ("Add todo", "Sign in"), not vague labels
   443→  ("Submit", "OK").
   444→- **No happy talk**: Every word earns its place or gets cut. No introductory paragraphs
   445→  that say nothing useful.
   446→- **Design for scanning, not reading**: Users don't read pages, they scan. Clear
   447→  headings, short text, prominent calls to action, no walls of text.
   448→- **Cut the text, then cut it again**: If you wrote a paragraph, ask "can I say this
   449→  in half the words?"
   450→
   451→### Domain Language and Modeling Conventions
   452→- Code uses the ubiquitous language of the domain. "Complete a task" ->
   453→  `complete_task()`, not `update_status()`.
   454→- Domain types are explicit -- no primitive obsession. Every domain concept gets its
   455→  own type, even if it is "just a string" or "just a UUID" underneath.
   456→- Newtypes are constructed only through validated factory methods (the `parse`
   457→  pattern). You cannot create a `TodoTitle` from an arbitrary string without going
   458→  through validation.
   459→- Every `is_*` boolean flag is a candidate for an enum. Ask: "what states does this
   460→  represent?" and model them explicitly.
   461→- Maintain a domain glossary in `docs/glossary.md` mapping business terms to types:
   462→
   463→  | Domain Term | Rust Type | Notes |
   464→  |-------------|-----------|-------|
   465→  | User | `User` | Authenticated user with valid credentials |
   466→  | Email | `ValidatedEmail` | Constructed via `parse`, not `new` |
   467→  | Todo item | `TodoItem` (enum) | `Pending` or `Completed` variant |
   468→  | Todo title | `TodoTitle` | Non-empty, max length, validated at construction |
   469→
   470→### Observability
   471→
   472→**Structured logging with `tracing`:**
   473→- Request ID (UUID) attached to every request via middleware.
   474→- Span fields: `request_id`, `method`, `uri`, `status_code`.
   475→- Application-level spans for significant operations: `register_user`,
   476→  `authenticate`, `create_todo`, etc.
   477→- Use `#[tracing::instrument]` for service functions.
   478→- Structured fields on errors: what failed, why, relevant IDs.
   479→
   480→**Log level conventions:**
   481→| Level | When to use |
   482→|-------|-------------|
   483→| `ERROR` | Something is broken, needs human attention |
   484→| `WARN` | Unexpected but handled (e.g., invalid login attempt) |
   485→| `INFO` | Significant business events (user registered, todo created) |
   486→| `DEBUG` | Technical details useful for debugging |
   487→| `TRACE` | Very verbose (request/response bodies, SQL queries) |
   488→
   489→### Database Conventions
   490→- **Migrations**: SQLx migrations (`sqlx migrate`), numbered SQL files, version controlled.
   491→- **Compile-time checked queries**: `sqlx::query!` and `sqlx::query_as!` macros.
   492→- **Offline mode for CI**: `sqlx-data.json` generated via `cargo sqlx prepare`,
   493→  committed to version control.
   494→- **Connection pool**: `PgPool` shared via Axum state.
   495→- **UUIDs for primary keys**: `uuid` type in PostgreSQL, `Uuid` in Rust.
   496→- **Timestamps**: `timestamptz` in PostgreSQL, `chrono::DateTime<Utc>` or
   497→  `time::OffsetDateTime` in Rust.
   498→- **Naming conventions**: snake_case for tables and columns. Plural table names
   499→  (`users`, `todos`).
   500→
   501→### Configuration Management
   502→- Layered configuration: `base.yaml` (defaults) -> `local.yaml` (gitignored overrides)
   503→  -> environment variables (12-factor style).
   504→- **Fail fast**: Missing required configuration at startup panics with a clear message.
   505→  No silent defaults for critical settings (database URL, secret keys).
   506→- Separate configuration structs: `DatabaseSettings`, `ApplicationSettings`,
   507→  `AuthSettings`.
   508→
   509→### CI/CD Quality Gates
   510→
   511→Every commit must pass (in order):
   512→1. `cargo fmt --check`
   513→2. `cargo clippy -- -D warnings`
   514→3. `cargo test` (unit + integration)
   515→4. `npx playwright test` (browser-based e2e)
   516→5. `cargo audit` (dependency vulnerabilities)
   517→
   518→### Atomic Green Step Pipeline
   519→
   520→*Every change follows this pipeline. No shortcuts, no skipped steps.*
   521→
   522→1. **Write code** — the smallest meaningful change.
   523→2. **`cargo fmt --check`** — fix formatting if needed.
   524→3. **`cargo clippy -- -D warnings`** — fix warnings if needed.
   525→4. **`cargo test`** — fix failures if needed.
   526→5. **`npx playwright test`** — fix e2e failures if needed.
   527→6. **Refactor** — look for duplication, unclear names, or structural improvements
   528→   before committing. The Refactor step is not optional.
   529→7. **Glossary check** — verify new domain types/fields match `docs/glossary.md`. If
   530→   deviating, update the glossary first (with domain architect approval).
   531→8. **`git commit`** — commit locally. **Do NOT push yet.**
   532→9. **All 9 agents review and reach consensus** — Driver and Reviewers confirm the change
   533→   is correct. If concerns are raised, the Driver fixes them, re-runs steps 2–7, and
   534→   re-seeks consensus.
   535→10. **Once 9/9 consensus → `git push`.**
   536→11. **Wait for CI green** — run `gh run list --limit 1` and WAIT for CI to complete
   537→    green before beginning the next change. Never have more than 1 pending CI run. If
   538→    CI fails, fix it before pushing anything else.
   539→12. **Mini-retrospective** (1-minute checkpoint) — The **team** (not the coordinator,
   540→    not the project owner) conducts this. The Driver initiates and all 9 agents
   541→    participate. Questions: (a) Did we follow the pipeline? (b) Was the commit
   542→    atomic? (c) Any process improvements? The project owner may offer suggestions as
   543→    an outside observer, but all process decisions are the team's to make.
   544→13. **Only then begin the next change.**
   545→
   546→Additional rules:
   547→- **Consensus before push.** Code is reviewed locally before it reaches the remote. This
   548→  keeps the CI wait out of the feedback loop.
   549→- **If CI fails, fix it before writing new code.** A red CI is the team's top priority.
   550→  No new features, no new tests, no refactoring until CI is green again.
   551→- **One Driver at a time.** Only the designated Driver may modify files. Non-driver agents
   552→  MUST NOT use Edit, Write, or Bash tools that modify files. Violation is treated as a
   553→  process failure.
   554→
   555→### Dependency Management
   556→
   557→Before adding a new crate:
   558→- Is it well-maintained? (Recent commits, responsive maintainers)
   559→- Is it widely used? (Downloads, reverse dependencies)
   560→- Compatible license? (MIT/Apache-2.0 preferred)
   561→- Can we avoid it? (Prefer stdlib or existing dependencies)
   562→
   563→### Documentation
   564→- **Code comments**: Only where "why" is not obvious from the code.
   565→- **ADRs**: For significant technical decisions. Format: context, decision,
   566→  consequences. Stored in `docs/adr/`.
   567→- **Domain glossary**: `docs/glossary.md` mapping business terms to code.
   568→- **README**: Practical -- how to set up, run tests, run the app.

   572→## 4. Disagreement Resolution
   573→
   574→### In the Mob (Day-to-Day)
   575→1. State the disagreement clearly: "I think X, you think Y."
   576→2. Each side explains their reasoning briefly (< 2 minutes each).
   577→3. If possible, **try both and let the code decide** -- write a quick test or prototype.
   578→4. If one approach is simpler, prefer it (smallest viable decision).
   579→5. If still stuck after 5 minutes, the mob votes. Majority wins for day-to-day decisions.
   580→   The dissenter can say "I disagree but won't block."
   581→
   582→### For Significant Decisions (Consensus Protocol)
   583→1. Any team member raises the decision point with a proposal and reasoning.
   584→2. Each team member shares their perspective (concerns, alternatives, support).
   585→3. Discussion continues until:
   586→   - **Consensus**: Everyone agrees or consents ("I can live with this").
   587→   - **Compromise**: A synthesized solution addresses all concerns.
   588→   - **Stand-aside**: "I disagree but won't block" -- recorded and respected.
   589→4. If no consensus after **10 rounds** of substantive discussion, **escalate to the
   590→   project owner** with:
   591→   - Options considered
   592→   - Each member's position
   593→   - Trade-offs
   594→   - Team recommendation (if possible)
   595→
   596→### When to Involve the Project Owner
   597→- Consensus deadlock after 10 rounds
   598→- Any proposed change to MVP scope
   599→- Questions about target user or business context the team cannot answer
   600→- Major architectural pivots that significantly change the timeline
   601→- Brief status updates at milestones (keep the owner informed, don't go dark)
   602→
   603→### Principles
   604→- Disagree with the idea, not the person.
   605→- Once decided, **disagree and commit** -- no undermining, no "I told you so."
   606→- Revisit decisions when new information emerges (from tests, users, or production).
   607→- **Write it down**: Major decisions recorded as Architecture Decision Records (ADRs)
   608→  in `docs/adr/`.

   611→
   612→## 5. Architectural Principles
   613→
   614→1. **Simplicity first**: Choose the simplest approach that could work. Add complexity
   615→   only when tests or user feedback demand it. "Could this be simpler?" is always a
   616→   valid question.
   617→2. **Server-side state**: The server is the source of truth for both data and
   618→   presentation. The browser renders what the server sends. No client-side state
   619→   management.
   620→3. **Pure domain core**: Domain logic is pure functions and types with no I/O. Side
   621→   effects live at the boundaries. This makes the domain trivially testable.
   622→4. **Make illegal states unrepresentable**: Use the type system to prevent bugs at
   623→   compile time. Newtypes, enums, and validated types carry proof of correctness.
   624→5. **Production-ready from day one**: Observability, error handling, and structured
   625→   logging from the first line of code -- not bolted on later.
   626→6. **Accessibility is a quality, not a feature**: Every feature is accessible from the
   627→   moment it is built. Inaccessible is broken. ~15-20% of users have some form of
   628→   disability -- that is not an edge case.
   629→7. **Progressive enhancement**: HTML works -> CSS makes it beautiful -> JS makes it
   630→   delightful. Each layer enhances without being required by the layers above.
   631→8. **Conventions over cleverness**: Follow established patterns. A login form should
   632→   look like every other login form. Don't make the user think. Design for scanning,
   633→   not reading. Cut unnecessary words. When in doubt, simplify.
   634→9. **Continuous testing**: TDD keeps us honest. The compiler is our first test. We
   635→   never skip the red step. Tests read like behavior specifications.
   636→10. **Incremental design**: Let the design emerge from tests and feedback. Don't
   637→    over-design upfront. Make the code a little better before adding a feature (Tidy
   638→    First), but don't redesign the universe. Ship early, learn fast.
   639→11. **Walking skeleton first**: Our first milestone is the thinnest possible slice
   640→    through the entire stack -- a user can register, log in, see an empty todo list,
   641→    add one item, and log out. Everything else builds on top.
   642→12. **Validate at the boundary, trust the types**: Input validation happens once, at
   643→    the system boundary. It produces typed values that carry proof of validity.
   644→    The interior of the system trusts its own types.

   647→
   648→## 6. Retrospective Cadence
   649→
   650→All team members are AI agents operating continuously. Time-based retros (weekly,
   651→bi-weekly) do not apply. Instead, retros are triggered by events.
   652→
   653→- **After each feature**: Retro immediately after completing a feature. What worked?
   654→  What didn't? What should we change?
   655→- **After significant incidents**: If a build breaks, a major refactor goes sideways,
   656→  or the team gets stuck for an extended period, retro after resolution.
   657→- **Format**: Simple Start/Stop/Continue.
   658→- **Action items**: Every retro produces at most 2-3 concrete action items. These are
   659→  tracked and checked at the next retro.
   660→- **Agreements are living**: These team agreements can be updated at any retro if the
   661→  team reaches consensus.
   662→- **Usability testing**: Aim for informal testing -- three users, 20 minutes each --
   663→  at natural breakpoints. Even watching one person use the app for 5 minutes reveals
   664→  more than a week of discussion.
   665→- **Post-CI checkpoint**: After each CI build (pass or fail), the **team** conducts
   666→  this checkpoint — NOT the coordinator, NOT the project owner. The Driver initiates
   667→  and all 9 agents participate: (a) Did we follow the pipeline? (b) Was the commit
   668→  atomic? (c) Any process improvements? This is a 1-minute checkpoint, not a full
   669→  retro. The project owner may offer suggestions as an outside observer/consultant,
   670→  but all process decisions are the team's to make.
   671→- **Deferred items**: Non-blocking a11y, design, and UX items are tracked in
   672→  `docs/deferred-items.md` and reviewed at each retrospective.

   739→**Recommendation**: The current 9-person team is sufficient for the MVP. Revisit at

   741→
